questions.prompt: Which statement about Google's 'duplicate content penalty' is correct?

choices.text: It's a major ranking penalty — having any duplicates can wipe out a site.
The duplicate content penalty doesn't exist. Google says explicitly that duplicates aren't a spam violation. The real cost is canonical confusion and wasted crawl budget.
It only applies to scraped content from other sites.
It's automatically applied to any site with HTTP and HTTPS versions.

questions.explanation: From Google's own SEO Starter Guide: 'Having duplicate content on your site is not a violation of our spam policies, but it can be a bad user experience and search engines might waste crawling resources on URLs that you don't even care about.' No penalty. Just inefficiency. The fix is canonicalization, not panic.

questions.prompt: Your ecommerce product page is reachable at /products/blue-shoes, plus dozens of variants like /products/blue-shoes?utm_source=email, ?sess=xyz, etc. What's the right consolidation strategy?

choices.text: Block all parameter variants in robots.txt.

Self-canonicalize: every variant has <link rel='canonical' href='https://example.com/products/blue-shoes'> pointing to the clean URL. Don't block them in robots.txt — Google needs to read the canonical hint.
Use 301 redirects from every variant to the clean URL.
Delete the clean URL and keep only one variant.

questions.explanation: Self-canonicalization is the right tool for tracking parameters and session IDs. 301 redirects break tracking. Blocking in robots.txt prevents Google from reading the canonical tag — making the problem worse. The rel=canonical points all variants at the clean URL, which becomes the one shown in results.
